title: Portrait Aspect Ratio Calculator - Image Dimensions
tagline: Calculate aspect ratios, scaled dimensions, and diagonal size for portrait photos and social media images across all platforms.
intro: Enter the width and height of your portrait image in pixels, inches, or centimeters to calculate the aspect ratio, diagonal, and scaled dimensions for any target ratio.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive numbers for width and height.
error.ratio: Target ratio must be in W:H format with positive numbers (e.g. 4:5).

about.heading: About the Portrait Aspect Ratio Calculator
about.body:
An aspect ratio describes the proportional relationship between the width and height of an image, expressed as a simplified fraction such as 4:5 or 9:16. In portrait photography and vertical-format content, the height is always greater than or equal to the width, making the image taller than it is wide. Understanding and controlling aspect ratios is fundamental to professional photography, graphic design, and social media content creation.

Different platforms mandate specific aspect ratios for optimal display. Instagram portrait posts use a 4:5 ratio (1080 × 1350 pixels), which maximizes vertical screen real estate in the feed. Instagram Stories and TikTok videos use 9:16 (1080 × 1920 pixels) to fill a phone screen completely. LinkedIn profile photos are 1:1 square (400 × 400 pixels). Facebook profile pictures display as circles from a 1:1 source. Pinterest vertical pins perform best at 2:3 (1000 × 1500 pixels). Knowing these ratios before shooting or exporting saves time in post-processing and ensures images are never awkwardly cropped.

The aspect ratio is calculated as the width divided by the height, then simplified by dividing both numbers by their greatest common divisor (GCD). For example, 1080 × 1350 pixels gives 1080/1350 = 0.8 = 4/5, so the simplified ratio is 4:5. The diagonal measurement is computed using the Pythagorean theorem: diagonal = √(width² + height²). This represents the distance from one corner of the image to the opposite corner and is useful for comparing image sizes independent of orientation.

Scaling to a target ratio preserves as much of the original image as possible. If your source image is 1200 × 1500 pixels and you want to fit it into a 4:5 frame, the calculator determines whether to scale by width or height to avoid cropping. When the source ratio matches the target, no scaling is needed. When they differ, the calculator shows the new dimensions that maintain the target ratio while keeping one dimension fixed.

Print photography uses different standards. A standard 4×6 print has a 2:3 ratio. An 8×10 print has a 4:5 ratio. Medium format portrait prints at 8×12 have a 2:3 ratio, while square prints at 4×4 have 1:1. The calculator works equally well with inch or centimeter inputs for print work, and with pixel inputs for digital and screen-based work.

When preparing portrait images for multiple platforms simultaneously, calculate each target ratio separately and crop from the center of the image to preserve the subject's face. Always work from the highest-resolution source available, as downscaling is lossless but upscaling introduces blur. The Portrait Aspect Ratio Calculator helps you plan crops before shooting, ensuring you capture enough pixels to satisfy the most demanding platform in your distribution workflow.

howto.heading: How to Use the Portrait Aspect Ratio Calculator
steps: Enter the width and height of your image in the corresponding fields and select the appropriate unit (pixels for digital, inches or cm for print).
Click Calculate to see the current aspect ratio, simplified ratio, diagonal size, and orientation classification.
To check how your image fits a specific platform, enter the target aspect ratio in W:H format (e.g. 4:5 for Instagram or 9:16 for Stories).
Review the scaled dimensions: the calculator shows the new width and height that match the target ratio while preserving one dimension of your original image.
Use the results to plan your crop in Photoshop, Lightroom, or any image editor — set the crop tool to the target ratio and align the frame over the subject.

items.q: What is the best aspect ratio for Instagram portrait posts?
items.a: The best aspect ratio for Instagram portrait posts is 4:5, which corresponds to 1080 × 1350 pixels. This ratio fills the maximum vertical space in the feed (Instagram allows up to 4:5 but crops anything taller). Using 4:5 gives your image 20% more screen height than a square post, making it more attention-grabbing without being truncated.

items.q: How do I calculate aspect ratio from pixel dimensions?
items.a: Divide the width by the height to get the decimal ratio, then find the simplified fraction by dividing both dimensions by their greatest common divisor. For 1080 × 1350: GCD is 270, so 1080/270 = 4 and 1350/270 = 5, giving a simplified ratio of 4:5. The calculator performs this automatically for any dimensions you enter.

items.q: What is the difference between aspect ratio and resolution?
items.a: Aspect ratio describes the shape of the image — the proportion of width to height — regardless of actual size. Resolution describes the total number of pixels. Two images can share the same 4:5 aspect ratio but have completely different resolutions: 400 × 500 and 1080 × 1350 are both 4:5 but the latter has over 7× more pixels. Higher resolution means more detail and larger print capability.

items.q: How do I resize an image to a specific aspect ratio without distortion?
items.a: To resize without distortion, crop the image to the target aspect ratio before scaling. First, determine the target ratio (e.g. 4:5). If your source image is wider relative to height, crop from the sides; if it is taller, crop from the top and/or bottom. Then scale the cropped image to the desired output dimensions. The calculator shows you the exact scaled dimensions needed so you can set your crop tool precisely.

items.q: Why does my image appear cropped on social media even after resizing?
items.a: Most social media platforms display images within a fixed aspect ratio frame in the feed, even if your uploaded image is a different shape. Instagram, for example, crops portrait images to a 4:5 display ratio in the feed even if the uploaded image is taller. Always upload at the platform's recommended ratio to avoid unexpected cropping. The examples section lists the recommended ratios for common platforms.

items.q: What aspect ratio should I use for portrait photography printing?
items.a: Common print aspect ratios are 2:3 for 4×6 and 8×12 prints, 4:5 for 8×10 prints, and 1:1 for square prints. Most digital cameras shoot at 2:3 (matching 35mm film proportions), so 4×6 prints require no cropping. For 8×10 prints, you need to crop from a 2:3 source to 4:5, which removes some of the top or bottom. Always verify the target ratio matches your print size before ordering to avoid unexpected crops at the lab.
